Decision-Making Power: High: Top 5 retailers (Walmart/Kroger) controlled 60% of volume.
Strength of Opposition: Moderate: UEP (United Egg Producers) lobbied against feasibility; argued $7-9B cost.
Type of Opposition: Economic: Industry argued supply chain transition costs and potential food inflation.
Visibility: High: HSUS undercover footage at Cal-Maine/others made cages visible.
Behaviour Change: Minimal: Corporate procurement policies (McDonalds/Starbucks) over consumer diet shift.
Primary Mechanism: Market: NGO leverage over CSR/Brand reputation; focus on 'responsible sourcing'.
Coalition & Non-Obvious Allies: Med: HSUS, THL, Open Wing Alliance. Non-obvious: Corporate CSR depts seeking brand protection.
Economic Disruption: Moderate: High capital investment needed for cage-free barns.
Speed/Scale: Fast: 300+ firms committed in 18mos. Large: 240M hens.
Public Starting Point: Latent: Public broadly negative once exposed to imagery.
Legal/Regulatory Landscape: None: Used private contractual power to mandate supply chain standards.
Narrative Dominance: Low: Industry lacked credible defense for battery cages post-exposure.

Decision-Making Power: Moderate: Targets retailers (Marks & Spencer/Compass) and breeders (Aviagen/Ross).
Strength of Opposition: High: Consolidated poultry industry (2 Sisters/Plukon) with deep gov ties.
Type of Opposition: Econ/Op: Industry asserts 30kg/m2 stocking and slower breeds threaten 'food security.'
Visibility: Low: Genetics/density are hidden/technical; visibility is restricted.
Behaviour Change: Moderate: Entire supply chain shift (genetics, light, stunning, space).
Primary Mechanism: Mixed: Market demand (Retailers) met with slow institutional resistance.
Coalition & Non-Obvious Allies: Med: CIWF, Open Wing Alliance. Lacks broad public health/eco alignment.
Economic Disruption: High: Disrupts core industrial efficiency (Feed Conversion Ratio).
Speed/Scale: Slow: 83M hens converted; significant stagnation in broad industrial scope.
Public Starting Point: Indifferent: Low public awareness of 'fast-growth' broiler genetics.
Legal/Regulatory Landscape: Fragmented: Mostly voluntary; lacks binding national standards.
Narrative Dominance: High: Industry successfully frames welfare as 'elite/luxury' vs 'affordable food.'
Event Log: 2017: Commitment launch; 2023: Shift to investor-led advocacy.
Final State: Mixed: Partial retailer adoption; industrial supply chain stalled.

Decision-Making Power: High: Single centralized CEO (Joel Manby) decision-making.
Strength of Opposition: High: SeaWorld used deep legal/PR resources to label film 'propaganda.'
Type of Opposition: Political/Legal: Aggressive lobbying against California Orca Protection Act.
Visibility: High: Blackfish (G. Cowperthwaite) made Tilikum’s history globally viral.
Behaviour Change: Minimal: Policy shift (stop breeding/shows) rather than mass behavior change.
Primary Mechanism: Cultural: Massive norm shift regarding 'entertainment' vs 'cruelty.'
Coalition & Non-Obvious Allies: Large: Filmmakers, Activists, former trainers (M. Simmons). Non-obvious: Schools/Families.
Economic Disruption: Moderate: Threatened core business model (orcas were primary draw).
Speed/Scale: Fast: 4.1% attendance drop within 1 year; policy pivot ensued.
Public Starting Point: Latent: Rapid shift from positive to critical post-media exposure.
Legal/Regulatory Landscape: Limited: Local state-level bans (California) post-social pressure.
Narrative Dominance: Low: Post-Blackfish, industry lost control of the 'entertainment' narrative.
Event Log: 2013: Blackfish release; 2014: Attendance drop; 2016: End of breeding.
Final State: Corporate policy adopted: End of captive orca breeding.

Decision-Making Power: High: Federal executive/legislative (Senate/Chamber of Deputies).
Strength of Opposition: Moderate: Agricultural lobbies defending traditional rural practices.
Type of Opposition: Political: Resistance used 'traditional culture' arguments against 'foreign' modernization.
Visibility: Moderate: Urban/Companion animal focus made issues visible and political.
Behaviour Change: Low: Legislative/constitutional mandate; limited individual behavior change.
Primary Mechanism: Policy: HSUS/Mexico & local groups (Igualdad Animal) targeted constitutional change.
Coalition & Non-Obvious Allies: Large: HSI/Mexico, Igualdad Animal, local political parties. Non-obvious: Urban health advocates.
Economic Disruption: Low: Limited direct economic impact compared to industrial sector.
Speed/Scale: Fast: Senate/Deputy unanimous vote; fast state ratification.
Public Starting Point: Supportive: Strong urban demand for legal modernization.
Legal/Regulatory Landscape: Supportive: Arts 3, 4, 73 amendments enabled federal oversight.
Narrative Dominance: Low: Modernization/Welfare narrative beat traditionalist arguments.

Decision-Making Power: Moderate: Targets regional giants (Carrefour/Grupo Exito) in emerging markets.
Strength of Opposition: Moderate: Varies by region; tied to local economic/inflationary concerns.
Type of Opposition: Econ/Ideological: Cites food insecurity/inflation for low-income segments.
Visibility: Low: Lack of visibility; NGOs rely on undercover investigations to trigger outreach.
Behaviour Change: Moderate: Significant logistics restructuring for supply chains.
Primary Mechanism: Market: Corporate pledges secured via investigative media/NGO pressure.
Coalition & Non-Obvious Allies: Med: Sinergia Animal, THL. Non-obvious: International investors (Banks for Animals).
Economic Disruption: Moderate: Required new infrastructure investment in developing markets.
Speed/Scale: Slow: Sequential, incremental commitments by regional retailers.
Public Starting Point: Indifferent: Primary concern is cost-of-living over animal welfare.
Legal/Regulatory Landscape: Mixed: Weak regulatory enforcement makes private policy primary.
Narrative Dominance: High: Industry control over 'food security' and 'affordable nutrition' framing.

Decision-Making Power: High: DEFRA/UK Parliament (Animal Welfare (Livestock Exports) Act 2024).
Strength of Opposition: Moderate: NFU (National Farmers' Union) argued against loss of market/logistics.
Type of Opposition: Political/Econ: NFU lobbied on 'food security' and abattoir capacity constraints.
Visibility: High: CIWF, OneKind, Viva! used decades of footage of cramped conditions.
Behaviour Change: Minimal: Legislative ban on export for slaughter/fattening, not breeding.
Primary Mechanism: Policy: Long-term lobbying following Brexit-enabled legislative freedom.
Coalition & Non-Obvious Allies: Large: CIWF, OneKind, Ayrshire Animal Save. Non-obvious: Local community groups.
Economic Disruption: Moderate: Forced structural adjustment in livestock logistics/slaughter.
Speed/Scale: Slow: Decades of grinding; rapid post-2021 as border control issues hit.
Public Starting Point: Latent: High public support for 'high welfare' post-Brexit.
Legal/Regulatory Landscape: Supportive: Brexit removed EU single-market trade barriers to national law.
Narrative Dominance: Low: Industry lost control; 'cruel trade' became the dominant narrative.
Event Log: 2017: Campaign kickoff; 2024: Act received Royal Assent.

Decision-Making Power: High: Cabinet/Minister of Agriculture (Andrew Hoggard).
Strength of Opposition: High: National/ACT Coalition govt pushed for repeal; industry lobbied for 'Gold Standard.'
Type of Opposition: Political/Econ: Industry claimed potential for 'Gold Standard' mitigation; govt pushed for repeal in 2024.
Visibility: High: Gulf Livestock 1 disaster (2020) and Heron Review made risks public.
Behaviour Change: Minimal: Legislative ban on maritime export of livestock for slaughter.
Primary Mechanism: Policy: SPCA/Green Party leveraged public outcry and 'cruelty' framing.
Coalition & Non-Obvious Allies: Large: SPCA, Green Party. Non-obvious: Concerned farmers supporting equal welfare.
Economic Disruption: High: Threatens industry revenue model for export-focused livestock sector.
Speed/Scale: Fast: 2022 Act passed; 2026 govt abandonment of repeal plan.
Public Starting Point: Supportive: Strong national identity as 'clean/green' and high welfare.
Legal/Regulatory Landscape: Supportive: 1999 Animal Welfare Act provided the regulatory vehicle.
Narrative Dominance: Low: Govt failed to frame 'Gold Standard' as a credible alternative.
Event Log: 2020: Gulf Livestock 1 disaster; 2022: Act passed; 2026: Repeal abandoned.

Decision-Making Power: High: DEFRA/Labour Govt (Steve Reed, Minister).
Strength of Opposition: Moderate: Culinary lobby and import industry (e.g., Gauthier/gastronomy influence).
Type of Opposition: Political: govt inaction despite pre-election pledges; trade deal secrecy.
Visibility: High: Animal Equality UK investigations (gavage/cages) made abuse visible.
Behaviour Change: Minimal: Legislative ban on imports (since production already illegal).
Primary Mechanism: Policy: Cross-party parliamentary pressure and celebrity campaigns.
Coalition & Non-Obvious Allies: Med: Animal Equality, celebrity backers (Ricky Gervais/Alexis Gauthier). Non-obvious: Michelin chefs.
Economic Disruption: Low: Niche luxury product; minimal macro-economic disruption.
Speed/Scale: Very Slow: Failed to materialize despite 2024 election pledges (failure mode).
Public Starting Point: Latent: Broad public support (9/10) due to 'torture' framing.
Legal/Regulatory Landscape: Complex: WTO/trade deal negotiations currently blocking implementation.
Narrative Dominance: High: Industry frames it as 'culinary tradition' vs 'hypocritical bans.'

Decision-Making Power: High: Federal Parliament (Turnbull Govt).
Strength of Opposition: Very High: Australian Christian Lobby, Marriage Alliance, Coalition for Marriage.
Type of Opposition: Ideological/Pol: Massive 'No' campaign focusing on 'religious freedom/traditional family.'
Visibility: High: National postal survey made it a constant, unavoidable social issue.
Behaviour Change: Moderate: Public required to participate in postal survey for legal change.
Primary Mechanism: Cultural: Massive mobilization of the 'Yes' movement (GetUp!, AME).
Coalition & Non-Obvious Allies: Very Large: GetUp!, AME, corporate sector. Non-obvious: Business leaders for diversity.
Economic Disruption: Low: No economic disruption; purely social/normative policy.
Speed/Scale: Fast: Rapid mobilization during the 3-month survey window.
Public Starting Point: Latent: Already majority support; campaign was about forcing a vote.
Legal/Regulatory Landscape: Supportive: Marriage Act 1961 was the target for amendment.
Narrative Dominance: Low: 'Yes' campaign narrative (equality/inclusion) overwhelmed 'No' arguments.
Event Log: 2017: Postal survey launched; 2017: 'Yes' victory; 2017: Act passed.

Decision-Making Power: High: Federal Health Minister (Nicola Roxon).
Strength of Opposition: Very High: BAT Australia, Philip Morris International, Imperial Tobacco.
Type of Opposition: Legal/Econ: $4.5M lobby spend; WTO/IP legal challenges (with Honduras/Cuba).
Visibility: High: Cancer Council's 'Plain Facts' and health warning campaigns.
Behaviour Change: Moderate: Shifts packaging aesthetic to limit brand appeal.
Primary Mechanism: Policy: Legislative change to Tobacco Plain Packaging Act 2011.
Coalition & Non-Obvious Allies: Large: Cancer Council Australia, public health experts. Non-obvious: None (mostly health-sector).
Economic Disruption: High: Threats to IP/brand equity; led to multi-year WTO dispute.
Speed/Scale: Fast: 2011 Act passed; full effect 2012; WTO win 2020.
Public Starting Point: Indifferent: Public supportive of anti-smoking measures generally.
Legal/Regulatory Landscape: Supportive: National Preventative Health Taskforce evidence-based approach.
Narrative Dominance: Low: Govt successfully framed brand appeal as a public health hazard.
Event Log: 2011: Act passed; 2012: Full effect; 2020: WTO win.

Decision-Making Power: Diffuse: Requires cultural shift across millions of individual workplaces/orgs.
Strength of Opposition: High: Entrenched patriarchal power in media, corporate, and political institutions.
Type of Opposition: Ideological/Pol: Backlash against 'cancel culture'; institutional resistance to legal liability.
Visibility: High: Viral social media mobilization (Alyssa Milano/Tarana Burke) made hidden abuse visible.
Behaviour Change: High: Fundamental shift in behavioral norms and consent culture in professional settings.
Primary Mechanism: Cultural: Norm shift leading to policy changes (HR procedures, legal reforms).
Coalition & Non-Obvious Allies: Large: Survivors, media figures. Non-obvious: Men’s advocacy groups (#IDidThat, #IWill).
Economic Disruption: Low: No macro-economic disruption; impact is internal to firm governance.
Speed/Scale: Fast: Rapid norm shift post-Weinstein (2017) fallout.
Public Starting Point: Indifferent: Issue was widespread but socially silenced until 2017.
Legal/Regulatory Landscape: Fragmented: Varied by jurisdiction (employment/tort law).
Narrative Dominance: Low: Movement successfully broke the industry's silence.
Final State: Ongoing: Norm shift, some legislative/HR policy changes.

Decision-Making Power: High: Government/Regulators (UNEP/Partnership for Clean Fuels & Vehicles).
Strength of Opposition: Moderate: Local oil dealers and lead producers resisting refinery upgrade costs.
Type of Opposition: Economic: Technical and financial resistance to refinery transitions in developing nations.
Visibility: Low: Health impacts (IQ loss, lead toxicity) are technical and long-term.
Behaviour Change: Minimal: Transition driven by regulatory mandate/fuel standards rather than consumer choice.
Primary Mechanism: Policy: Multilateral global phase-out led by UNEP.
Coalition & Non-Obvious Allies: Large: Global scientific/health community, UNEP, PCFV. Non-obvious: Private fuel retailers.
Economic Disruption: Moderate: Global cost of refinery upgrades ($2.45T economic benefit).
Speed/Scale: Slow: 20-year grinding transition to reach 100% phase-out in 2021.
Public Starting Point: Indifferent: Health impacts were not a 'visible' public priority.
Legal/Regulatory Landscape: Supportive: International treaties and national environmental standards.
Narrative Dominance: Low: Scientific evidence on health risks eventually silenced lead-lobbyists.

Decision-Making Power: High: Global garment brands (H&M, Inditex) holding procurement power.
Strength of Opposition: Moderate: Factory owners in BD initially resisted safety costs/oversight.
Type of Opposition: Economic: Pressure on production margins and 'fast fashion' cost models.
Visibility: High: 1,100+ deaths in building collapse created immediate moral crisis.
Behaviour Change: Moderate: Industrial safety/building standards (fire/structural safety).
Primary Mechanism: Market/Policy: Legally binding agreement between brands and trade unions.
Coalition & Non-Obvious Allies: Large: IndustriALL, UNI Global, NGOs. Non-obvious: Institutional investors.
Economic Disruption: Moderate: High remediation costs but essential for continued sourcing.
Speed/Scale: Fast: Accord signed months after 2013 tragedy.
Public Starting Point: Supportive: Public outrage over worker deaths drove brand action.
Legal/Regulatory Landscape: Supportive: Institutional pressure created a 'de facto' law for signatory firms.
Narrative Dominance: Low: Brands forced to accept safety responsibility to save reputation.

Decision-Making Power: High: Local municipal governments and US state/federal legislatures.
Strength of Opposition: Very High: Police unions (FOP) and political 'law and order' factions.
Type of Opposition: Political: Co-optation of reforms; strong counter-narrative of 'defunding' risk.
Visibility: High: Visual evidence of George Floyd's murder mobilized global protest.
Behaviour Change: Moderate: Legislative policing reform (Breathe Act, use-of-force policies).
Primary Mechanism: Policy: Legislative lobbying and mass protests.
Coalition & Non-Obvious Allies: Very Large: M4BL, grassroots orgs. Non-obvious: Sports leagues/corporate donors.
Economic Disruption: Moderate: Requires shift in municipal budget allocations.
Speed/Scale: Slow: Contested wins; some cities reformed, others saw backlash/regression.
Public Starting Point: Latent: Growing awareness of state violence against minorities.
Legal/Regulatory Landscape: Fragmented: US federalism makes local reform a patchwork.
Narrative Dominance: High: 'Law and order' narrative persists as a powerful opposition tool.

Decision-Making Power: High: EU Council/Parliament (Brussels Effect).
Strength of Opposition: High: Business associations (e.g., BusinessEurope) citing 'competitiveness.'
Type of Opposition: Economic: Lobbying against 'administrative burden' and potential liability.
Visibility: Low: Supply chain impacts are complex/geographically distant.
Behaviour Change: Moderate: Mandatory human rights/environmental due diligence.
Primary Mechanism: Policy: EU regulatory mandate ('Brussels Effect').
Coalition & Non-Obvious Allies: Large: NGOs, environmental groups. Non-obvious: EU-based SMEs wanting level playing fields.
Economic Disruption: High: Threatens established lean supply chain models with liability.
Speed/Scale: Slow: 2 years of intense negotiation; implementation still evolving.
Public Starting Point: Indifferent: Technical nature limits public awareness.
Legal/Regulatory Landscape: Supportive: EU single-market legislation as the regulatory vehicle.
Narrative Dominance: High: Industry successfully reframed the directive as a 'competitiveness risk.'

Decision-Making Power: High: Federal Parliament (Gillard/Abbott Govts).
Strength of Opposition: Low: No institutional opposition; broad political consensus eventually formed.
Type of Opposition: Political: Initial fiscal hesitation replaced by bipartisan support.
Visibility: Low: Disability issues were previously hidden; campaign 'Humanized' the need.
Behaviour Change: Minimal: Structural/Legislative shift (creation of NDIS).
Primary Mechanism: Policy: Grassroots organizing and strategic political lobbying.
Coalition & Non-Obvious Allies: Very Large: National Disability & Carer Alliance, grass-roots families.
Economic Disruption: Low: High fiscal cost but seen as a national social investment.
Speed/Scale: Fast: 2-year campaign led to 2013 rollout.
Public Starting Point: Latent: Strong public sympathy for the 'dignity' of people with disabilities.
Legal/Regulatory Landscape: Supportive: Political climate favored a major social reform.
Narrative Dominance: Low: Campaign successfully established NDIS as a 'moral imperative.'

Decision-Making Power: High: Federal Govt (Immigration Dept).
Strength of Opposition: Very High: Institutional/Political 'Border Security' consensus (both sides).
Type of Opposition: Ideological/Pol: 'Border protection' frames refugees as security threats.
Visibility: Moderate: Protests and media leaks (Palm Inquiry) made conditions visible.
Behaviour Change: Minimal: Political and administrative.
Primary Mechanism: Policy: Deep institutional capture of migration policy.
Coalition & Non-Obvious Allies: Large: AMA, refugee advocate groups, legal bodies. Non-obvious: Medical professionals.
Economic Disruption: Low: High cost, but framed as essential for national security.
Speed/Scale: Very Slow: 30+ years; persistent failure mode (policy survives human rights protests).
Public Starting Point: Hostile: Public often manipulated to prioritize 'fear' over 'dignity.'
Legal/Regulatory Landscape: Blocking: Legislation explicitly maintains mandatory detention status.
Narrative Dominance: High: 'Stop the Boats' narrative dominates public and political discourse.

Decision-Making Power: High: Federal Government of Nigeria (Police Force).
Strength of Opposition: Very High: Security forces and state-aligned groups.
Type of Opposition: Political/Operational: Violent state suppression (Lekki tollgate massacre).
Visibility: High: Social media documentation of police brutality reached global scale.
Behaviour Change: Moderate: Disbandment of specific SARS unit.
Primary Mechanism: Mixed: Mass street mobilization met with violent state pushback.
Coalition & Non-Obvious Allies: Large: Gen Z activists, diaspora. Non-obvious: International rights bodies.
Economic Disruption: Low: Minimal economic disruption to the state.
Speed/Scale: Fast: SARS dissolved in days; protests then crushed by military force.
Public Starting Point: Supportive: Strong national youth demand for good governance.
Legal/Regulatory Landscape: Blocking: State used force to bypass accountability mechanisms.
Narrative Dominance: Low: Protesters controlled the 'End SARS' narrative until mass violence.
Event Log: 2017: Campaign launch; 2020: SARS disbanded; 2020: Protest suppression.
Final State: Contested: SARS disbanded, but systemic reform failed.

Decision-Making Power: Moderate: Federal Govt and large landholders.
Strength of Opposition: Very High: Bolsonaro-era gov, agribusiness lobbies (Ruralistas).
Type of Opposition: Economic/Pol: 'Ruralista' power in Congress; historical stigma of land occupations.
Visibility: Moderate: Food production visibility (arroz orgânico) helped outreach.
Behaviour Change: Moderate: Shift from 'land occupier' to 'food producer.'
Primary Mechanism: Cultural: Narrative reframe: Land reform as 'sustainable food production.'
Coalition & Non-Obvious Allies: Medium: Urban progressives, small farmers. Non-obvious: Urban middle-class consumers.
Economic Disruption: High: Challenges the agro-industrial export business model.
Speed/Scale: Moderate: Decade-long shift toward 'food-based' organizing.
Public Starting Point: Hostile: Stigma of illegal occupation/radicalism.
Legal/Regulatory Landscape: Complex: Legal protections vs. active political repression.
Narrative Dominance: Moderate: Movement successfully reframed itself as a 'food provider.'
Event Log: 2010s: Rebrand to food production; 2020s: Ongoing lobbying.
Final State: Ongoing: Narrative shifted, land reform contested.

Decision-Making Power: High: Global fashion brands (Zara, Mango, Levi's).
Strength of Opposition: Low: Industry brands were sensitive to consumer 'brand safety' concerns.
Type of Opposition: Economic: Cost of supply chain chemical monitoring/transparency.
Visibility: Low: Toxicity in wastewater is hidden; NGOs needed investigative reporting.
Behaviour Change: Moderate: Supply chain procurement and chemical management standards.
Primary Mechanism: Market: Leverage over brand reputation.
Coalition & Non-Obvious Allies: Medium: Greenpeace, supply chain partners. Non-obvious: Investors/shareholders.
Economic Disruption: Moderate: Required significant upgrades in chemical monitoring.
Speed/Scale: Moderate: 10-year transition; voluntary but widespread adoption by 'vanguard' brands.
Public Starting Point: Indifferent: Fashion consumption was disconnected from chemical environmental impact.
Legal/Regulatory Landscape: None: Relied on voluntary corporate pledges rather than law.
Narrative Dominance: Low: Brands were vulnerable to exposure of 'toxic' labels.
